perturbation.original_text: Each Party specifically acknowledges that use or disclosure of the Confidential Information may be subject to U.S. Government’s export laws and regulatio ns, included but not limited to, the International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR) and the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) and warrants that such Party shall make no use or disclosure of the Confidential Information contrary to such laws or othe r applicable laws. Any Confidential Information disclosed, by either P arty, will be marked with its control status under the regulations. In the case of information disclosed verbally the control status will be given prior to discussions and the subseque nt memo will be marked wi th that status. The Receiving Party certifies that no portion of the Confidential Information will be exported or transferred, whether directly or indirectly, without first complying strictly and fully with all export controls that may be imposed on such information by the United States government or any country or organization or nations within whose jurisdiction the Receiving Party operates or does business. The Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party harmles s against all claims, demands, damages, costs, fines, penalties, attorneys’ fees, and all other expenses arising from failure of the Receiving Party to comply with this Section 3 or applicable export control laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that use or disclosure of the Confidential Information may be subject to export laws and regulations. Any Confidential Information disclosed will be marked with its control status. The Receiving Party will comply with export controls that may be imposed on such information. The Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party harmless against all claims arising from failure of the Receiving Party to comply with this Section 3 or applicable export control laws and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly mentions ITAR and EAR, providing a clear warning and requiring strict compliance. The modified text removes the specific reference to ITAR and EAR and weakens the compliance requirement. The law mandates that companies take all necessary steps to prevent unauthorized export of controlled items, which includes explicitly mentioning ITAR and EAR. By removing that level of explicit instruction, the contract puts the disclosing party at risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the adherence to export regulations specifically related to ITAR (International Traffic in Arms Regulations), which are mandated by 22 CFR § 123.22 regarding voluntary disclosure of information. It imposes requirements of US companies when disclosing technical data to foreign parties. By reducing the explicitness and scope of compliance, the modified text falls short of what is required to ensure that the disclosing party has taken necessary precautions to avoid unauthorized export of technical data.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 22 CFR § 123.22 - Filing, retention, and return of export licenses and filing of export information. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 22 CFR § 123.22 - Filing, retention, and return of export licenses and filing of export information. CFR prev | next § 123.22 Filing, retention, and return of export licenses and filing of export information. (a) Any export, as defined in this subchapter, of a defense article controlled by this subchapter, to include defense articles transiting the United States, requires the electronic reporting of export information. The reporting of the export information shall be to the U.S. Customs and Border Protection using its electronic system(s), or directly to the Directorate of Defense Trade Controls (DDTC), as appropriate. Before the export of any hardware, via a license or other authorization, the DDTC registered applicant/exporter, or an agent acting on the filer's behalf, must electronically submit export information to U.S. Customs and Border Protection, unless electronic reporting is unavailable, in which case U.S. Customs and Border Protection will issue instructions ( see paragraph (b) of this section). In addition to electronically providing the export information to U.S. Customs and Border Protection before export, all mandatory supporting documentation (e.g., attachments, certifications, proof of filing in U.S. Customs and Border Protection's system(s) such as the Internal Transaction Number (ITN)) must be submitted electronically, unless electronic reporting of such information is unavailable, in which case U.S. Customs and Border Protection will issue instructions. (1) If necessary, an export may be made through a port other than the one designated on the license if the exporter complies with the procedures established by U.S. Customs and Border Protection. (2) When a defense article is temporarily exported from the United States and
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the explicit adherence to export regulations, specifically ITAR and EAR, which the original text clearly stated. The Law Explanation states that by removing the explicitness and scope of compliance, the modified text falls short of ensuring that the disclosing party has taken necessary precautions to avoid unauthorized export of technical data. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions 22 CFR § 123.22, which pertains to filing and reporting export information, reinforcing the importance of adherence to export regulations. While the snippet itself does not explicitly say ITAR must be named, it points to the necessity of following the correct export regulations, which the modified text makes less clear.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party hereby agrees that any Confidential Information received by it will be used solely for the Business Purpose, and that such information will be kept confidential by such Party; provided, however, that such Confidential Information may be disclosed to the Representatives of the Receiving Party on a need to know basis to the extent necessary to further the Business Purpose (it being understood that such Representatives shall be informed by the Receiving Party of the confidential nature of such information and shall be directed to treat such information confidentially and that the Rec eiving Party shall take all reasonable measures to restrain Quest Aircraft Company, LLC Page 2 of 6 1200 Turbine Drive, Sandpoint, ID 83864 p: (208) 263 -1111 f: (208) 263 -1511 Version: SNDA 1.0 Date: Dec. 2015 its Representatives from the unauthorized use or disclosure of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information). In addition, each Party , as a Receiving Party, shall: a) not directly or indirectl y cause, permit or enable the disclosure, publication, transfer, misappropriation or revealing to any person or entity not s pecifically permitted hereunder, of any of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information without the clear and express prior writt en consent of a duly authorized officer of the Disclosing Party ; b.) take such precautions, contractual or otherwise, as shall be required to keep the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information strictly secret and confidential and will be responsible to the Disclosing Party for (i) its Representatives ’ use and disclosure of the Confidential Information; and (ii) any third party’s use and disclosure of the Confidential Information that the Receiving Party provides to such third party in accordance with thi s Agreement. c.) protect the Confidential Information disclosed to such Party by the other Disclosing Party with the higher of the standard of care which such party uses to protect its own proprietary information or the standard of care which a prudent business person would exercise to protect valuable proprietary information, and
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party hereby agrees that any Confidential Information received by it will be used solely for the Business Purpose, and that such information will be kept confidential by such Party; provided, however, that such Confidential Information may be disclosed to the Representatives of the Receiving Party to the extent necessary to further the Business Purpose. In addition, each Party , as a Receiving Party, shall: a) not directly or indirectl y cause, permit or enable the disclosure, publication, transfer, misappropriation or revealing to any person or entity not s pecifically permitted hereunder, of any of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information without the prior writt en consent of the Disclosing Party ; b.) take reasonable precautions to keep the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information secret and confidential and will be responsible to the Disclosing Party for its Representatives’ use and disclosure of the Confidential Information. c.) protect the Confidential Information disclosed to such Party by the other Disclosing Party with the standard of care which such party uses to protect its own proprietary information and
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes the requirement that representatives are informed of the confidential nature of the information and directed to treat it confidentially. It also removes the specific requirement to take 'all reasonable measures' to restrain representatives from unauthorized use or disclosure and softens the precautions to 'reasonable precautions'. It weakens the requirement that the consent must be from 'a duly authorized officer of the Disclosing Party'. These changes weaken the confidentiality protections and can contradict trade secret laws. Under Idaho trade secret law reasonable efforts must be made to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: Idaho Code § 48-803 allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. To qualify for trade secret protection, reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy must be demonstrated. The modified text weakens the confidentiality obligations by removing the requirement to inform representatives of the confidential nature of the information and by softening the precautions required, and not requiring a 'duly authorized officer' to permit disclosure, which may impact the ability to obtain injunctive relief if the information is later misappropriated.

(1) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. Universal Citation: ID Code § 48-803 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 48-803. DAMAGES. (1) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (2) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (1) of this section. perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Idaho trade secret law because it weakens the 'reasonable efforts' required to maintain secrecy. The original text mandated informing representatives of confidentiality and taking 'all reasonable measures' to restrain unauthorized use, while the changed text omits the former and softens the latter to 'reasonable precautions'. Furthermore, it weakens the requirement of consent from a 'duly authorized officer'. According to the Law Explanation, Idaho Code § 48-803 allows injunctive relief for trade secret misappropriation, but this requires demonstrating 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights that these omissions weaken confidentiality protections and can contradict trade secret laws.

perturbation.original_text: Within ten (10) calendar days of written request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall return to the Disclosing Party, or at the Disclosing Party's option, shall destroy, any physical and written records containing such Confidential Information, whether such physical and written records were sup plied or made available by the Disclosing Party or were prepared by the R ecipient or its Representatives from information sup plied or made available by the Disclosing Party. In such event, the R ecipient shall certify in writing to the Disclosing P arty that such physical or written materials have been returned o r destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall return any physical and written records containing such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes the 10-day deadline for returning or destroying confidential information, the option for the disclosing party to request destruction, and the requirement for written certification of return or destruction. These removals weaken the protection of trade secrets. Under Idaho trade secret law, reasonable steps must be taken to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: Idaho Code § 48-801 defines 'trade secret' and requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain its secrecy. By removing the specific deadline, the destruction option, and the certification requirement, the contract now fails to provide reasonable measures to safeguard confidential information, potentially undermining the trade secret status of that information, and losing a party their right to protect it under the law.

As used in this chapter unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquire

As used in this chapter unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes essential components required to protect trade secrets under Idaho law. Specifically, the removal of the 10-day deadline, the option for destruction at the Disclosing Party's request, and the written certification requirement significantly weakens the measures taken to maintain secrecy. Idaho Code § 48-801, as indicated in both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, defines misappropriation and implicitly requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By eliminating these protective measures, the contract creates a higher risk of misappropriation because the Receiving Party has less stringent obligations to safeguard the Confidential Information.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any dispute with respect to the subject matter of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall recover, in addition to any other d amages assessed, its reasonable attorneys' fees and costs incurred in litigating, arbitrating, or otherwise settling or resolving such dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any dispute with respect to the subject matter of this Agreement, each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides for the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees and costs. The modified text eliminates this provision, requiring each party to bear its own costs regardless of who prevails. This can conflict with Idaho Code Section 12-120(3) which pertains to attorney fees for commercial transactions. If the Supplier Agreement falls under that law, they are entitled to attorney fees.
perturbation.law_explanation: Idaho Code § 12-120(3) discusses attorney fees in civil actions, specifically stating that in any commercial transaction (as defined), the prevailing party shall be awarded reasonable attorney's fees. By explicitly stating that each party bears their own fees, the modified contract contradicts Idaho law if this agreement is derived from a commercial transaction. The agreement relates to the 'manufacture, sale, and support of Build -to-Print Goods,' which can be considered a commercial transaction.

Search Idaho Statutes Search Statutes Search Constitution pecnv.out TITLE 12 COSTS AND MISCELLANEOUS MATTERS IN CIVIL ACTIONS CHAPTER 1 COSTS 12-120. Attorney’s fees in civil actions. (1) Except as provided in subsections (3) and (4) of this section, in any action where the amount pleaded is thirty-five thousand dollars ($35,000) or less, there shall be taxed and allowed to the prevailing party, as part of the costs of the action, a reasonable amount to be fixed by the court as attorney’s fees. For the plaintiff to be awarded attorney’s fees, for the prosecution of the action, written demand for the payment of such claim must have been made on the defendant not less than ten (10) days before the commencement of the action; provided, that no attorney’s fees shall be allowed to the plaintiff if the court finds that the defendant tendered to the plaintiff, prior to the commencement of the action, an amount at least equal to ninety-five percent

Previous Next 12-120. ATTORNEY’S FEES IN CIVIL ACTIONS. (1) Except as provided in subsections (3) and (4) of this section, in any action where the amount pleaded is thirty-five thousand dollars ($35,000) or less, there shall be taxed and allowed to the prevailing party, as part of the costs of the action, a reasonable amount to be fixed by the court as attorney’s fees. For the plaintiff to be awarded attorney’s fees, for the prosecution of the action, written demand for the payment of such claim must have been made on the defendant not less than ten (10) days before the commencement of the action; provided, that no attorney’s fees shall be allowed to the plaintiff if the court finds that the defendant tendered to the plaintiff, prior to the commencement of the action, an amount at least equal to ninety-five percent (95%) of the amount awarded to the plaintiff. (2) The provisions of subsection (1) of this section shall also apply to any counterclaims, cross-claims or third party claims which may be filed after the initiation of the original action. Except that a ten (10) day written demand letter shall not be required in the case of a counterclaim. (3) In any civil action to recover on an open account, account stated, note, bill, negotiable instrument, guaranty, or contract relating to the purchase or sale of goods, wares, merchandise, or services and in any
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts Idaho Code § 12-120(3) as explained in the Law Explanation, which states that in commercial transactions, the prevailing party shall be awarded reasonable attorney's fees. The changed text stipulates that each party bears its own fees, directly negating the law's provision for awarding fees to the prevailing party in commercial transactions. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically Section 12-120, subsection (3), reinforces this by mentioning actions to recover on a contract relating to the purchase or sale of goods, wares, merchandise, or services, suggesting this agreement could fall under said subsection.
